content_markdown: An ideal gas expands isothermally from a state X to a new state of volume $V$. The work done by the gas is $W$. Which of the following is correct for an adiabatic expansion of the gas from state X to a new state of volume $V$?

diagram_metadata.description: A table showing four options (A, B, C, D) with columns for 'Change in internal energy' and 'Work done'.

options.label: A
options.text: Change in internal energy: $\Delta U > 0$; Work done: greater than $W$

options.label: B
options.text: Change in internal energy: $\Delta U < 0$; Work done: greater than $W$

options.label: C
options.text: Change in internal energy: $\Delta U > 0$; Work done: less than $W$

options.label: D
options.text: Change in internal energy: $\Delta U < 0$; Work done: less than $W$

vibe_explanation: In an adiabatic expansion, the gas does work at the expense of its internal energy ($Q=0$, so $\Delta U = -W_{by}$). Since the gas expands, work is positive, meaning $\Delta U$ must be negative ($\Delta U < 0$). Furthermore, on a $P-V$ diagram, an adiabatic curve is steeper than an isothermal curve. Starting from the same point X and expanding to the same final volume $V$, the pressure for the adiabatic process will always be lower than for the isothermal process. Since work is the area under the $P-V$ curve, the adiabatic work done will be less than the isothermal work $W$.
